f: Digitoxin vs Digoxin in HFrEF — key finding
b: DIGIT-HF trial: Digitoxin did NOT reduce composite of HF hospitalization + CV death vs placebo in HFrEF. Unlike DIG trial (digoxin reduced HF hospitalization), digitoxin showed no benefit. Both are cardiac glycosides but digitoxin has longer half-life and hepatic metabolism.

Digitoxin: hepatic metabolism, T½ 5-7 days, NO renal adjustment. Digoxin: renal clearance, T½ 36-48h, requires dose adjustment in CKD. Both inhibit Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase → ↑intracellular Ca²⁺ → positive inotropy.

f: Shield test (cfDNA) for colorectal cancer screening — sensitivity & specificity
b: ECLIPSE trial: cell-free DNA blood test (Shield/Guardant) detected CRC with 83% sensitivity and 90% specificity. For advanced adenomas sensitivity was only ~13%. FDA-approved 2024. Does NOT replace colonoscopy — complementary for patients who decline colonoscopy.

Colonoscopy: gold standard, 10yr intervals. FIT: annual, ~74% CRC sensitivity. Cologuard (stool DNA): 92% CRC sensitivity but lower specificity. cfDNA blood (Shield): 83% CRC sensitivity, 90% specificity. All positive non-colonoscopy tests require follow-up colonoscopy.

f: BALANCE trial — 7 vs 14 days for bloodstream infections
b: 7 days non-inferior to 14 days for uncomplicated BSI. Primary outcome: 90-day mortality — no difference. Exclusions: endocarditis, undrained abscess, prosthetic joint/valve infection, S. aureus BSI, severely immunocompromised. S. aureus BSI was EXCLUDED — always treat ≥4 weeks.

f: When NOT to shorten antibiotics for BSI (BALANCE exclusions)
b: Do NOT use 7-day course for: S. aureus bacteremia (needs 4-6 weeks), endocarditis, undrained deep abscess, prosthetic device infection, severe neutropenia, bone/joint infection. These require prolonged therapy regardless of clinical improvement.

f: Aspirin + OAC in stable coronary disease — AFIRE confirmed
b: Patients with chronic coronary syndrome (>1yr post-PCI) on OAC for AFib: adding aspirin INCREASES bleeding without reducing ischemic events. OAC monotherapy (rivaroxaban or DOAC) is preferred. Stop aspirin after 1yr post-PCI if on OAC for AFib.

Baxdrostat: aldosterone synthase inhibitor (CYP11B2). Novel mechanism for resistant/uncontrolled HTN. Phase 3: significant SBP reduction vs placebo (~10-12 mmHg). Key side effect: hyperkalemia (monitor K⁺). Targets aldosterone pathway without blocking mineralocorticoid receptor.

Resistant HTN: BP above goal despite ≥3 drugs (including diuretic) at optimal doses. Refractory HTN: BP above goal despite ≥5 drugs. Before diagnosing: confirm adherence, exclude white-coat effect, exclude secondary causes (RAS, pheo, Cushing, primary aldosteronism).

f: Apixaban for extended treatment of provoked VTE
b: Even provoked VTE (surgery, immobility, hormones) benefits from extended apixaban if risk factors persist. Extended treatment (2.5mg BID after initial 6mo) reduced recurrence ~80% vs placebo. Bleeding risk was low. Challenge: identifying which provoked VTE patients have ongoing risk.

f: Upadacitinib for Giant Cell Arteritis — SELECT-GCA trial
b: JAK inhibitor upadacitinib (15mg daily) achieved sustained remission in GCA, enabling faster steroid tapering. Phase 3: ~60% sustained remission vs ~26% placebo at week 52. Allows steroid dose reduction. Monitoring: infections, VTE, malignancy (JAK inhibitor class warnings).

First-line: high-dose prednisone 40-60mg + rapid taper. Steroid-sparing: tocilizumab (IL-6 inhibitor, FDA-approved). NEW: upadacitinib (JAK inhibitor). Methotrexate: modest steroid-sparing effect. Never delay steroids — risk of blindness from anterior ischemic optic neuropathy (AION).

f: First-line treatment of pulmonary sarcoidosis — prednisone vs MTX
b: FIRE trial: Methotrexate was non-inferior to prednisone as first-line for pulmonary sarcoidosis. MTX had fewer metabolic side effects (less weight gain, less glucose intolerance). Both improved FVC similarly at 24 weeks. MTX may be preferred first-line given better side-effect profile.

Treat when: progressive/symptomatic pulmonary disease, cardiac involvement, neuro involvement, hypercalcemia, eye disease unresponsive to topical therapy. Many patients need NO treatment — up to 2/3 resolve spontaneously. Löfgren syndrome (EN + bilateral hilar LAD + arthritis) has excellent prognosis.

STELLAR trial: Sotatercept (activin signaling inhibitor, TGF-β superfamily trap) added to background PAH therapy improved 6MWD, reduced NT-proBNP, improved WHO FC, and reduced clinical worsening events. First-in-class mechanism targeting disease pathobiology beyond vasodilation. Given subcutaneously q3weeks.

Endothelin receptor antagonists (bosentan, ambrisentan). PDE5 inhibitors (sildenafil, tadalafil). Prostacyclin pathway (epoprostenol, treprostinil, selexipag). sGC stimulator (riociguat). NEW: Sotatercept (activin trap). Combination therapy is standard. CCB only for vasoreactive patients (~5%).

f: ECST-2 trial — carotid stenosis: surgery vs medical therapy
b: Asymptomatic or low-risk symptomatic carotid stenosis: optimized medical therapy ALONE was as good as medical therapy + revascularization (CEA/CAS) at 2-year interim. Modern medical therapy (statins, BP control, antiplatelets) dramatically reduced stroke risk, challenging routine revascularization.

Symptomatic >70%: still consider CEA/CAS (per NASCET/ECST). Symptomatic 50-69%: moderate benefit from CEA (NNT ~15). Asymptomatic >60%: ECST-2 suggests optimal medical therapy may suffice. All patients: high-dose statin, BP <130/80, antiplatelet, smoking cessation, diabetes control.

1) T2DM (glycemic control). 2) HFrEF AND HFpEF (regardless of diabetes). 3) CKD with proteinuria (DAPA-CKD, CREDENCE). 4) Reduce CV events in T2DM with ASCVD. Key side effects: euglycemic DKA, UTI/genital mycotic infections, volume depletion.

Dabigatran → Idarucizumab (Praxbind). Rivaroxaban/Apixaban/Edoxaban → Andexanet alfa (Andexxa). If no specific agent available: 4-factor PCC. Do NOT use FFP for DOAC reversal (ineffective). Activated charcoal within 2hr of ingestion.

Sepsis = life-threatening organ dysfunction caused by dysregulated host response to infection. Organ dysfunction = SOFA score ≥2 points above baseline. qSOFA (screening): ≥2 of: RR≥22, altered mentation (GCS<15), SBP≤100. Septic shock = sepsis + vasopressors needed for MAP≥65 + lactate>2.

Stage 1: Cr ↑1.5-1.9× baseline OR ↑≥0.3mg/dL in 48h OR UO<0.5mL/kg/h for 6-12h. Stage 2: Cr ↑2.0-2.9× OR UO<0.5mL/kg/h for ≥12h. Stage 3: Cr ↑3× OR Cr≥4.0 OR initiation of RRT OR UO<0.3mL/kg/h for ≥24h OR anuria ≥12h.

Clinical signs of DVT: +3. PE most likely diagnosis: +3. HR>100: +1.5. Immobilization/surgery within 4wk: +1.5. Previous DVT/PE: +1.5. Hemoptysis: +1. Malignancy: +1. Score ≤4: PE unlikely → D-dimer. Score >4: PE likely → CTPA.

Exudate if ANY: 1) Pleural protein/serum protein >0.5. 2) Pleural LDH/serum LDH >0.6. 3) Pleural LDH >2/3 upper limit of normal serum LDH. All 3 negative = transudate (CHF, cirrhosis, nephrotic). Common trap: diuretic use can make transudates look like exudates.

Type 1 (AKI-HRS): rapid Cr doubling in <2 weeks, poor prognosis. Type 2: slow, gradual Cr rise with refractory ascites. Both: prerenal physiology without response to volume. Diagnosis: exclude other causes, no improvement after albumin challenge (1g/kg×2d). Treatment: albumin + terlipressin/midodrine+octreotide.

DKA: glucose usually 300-800, pH<7.3, bicarb<18, anion gap >12, ketones positive. Mainly T1DM. HHS: glucose >600 (often >1000), osmolality >320, minimal ketones, pH usually >7.3. Mainly T2DM elderly. Both: volume depletion, K⁺ monitoring critical (total body K⁺ depleted even if serum K⁺ is high).

Step 1: Serum osmolality (exclude pseudo-hyponatremia). Step 2: Urine osmolality (<100 = primary polydipsia). Step 3: Volume status — Hypovolemic (UNa<20: GI/skin loss; UNa>20: diuretics/adrenal). Euvolemic: SIADH, hypothyroid, cortisol deficiency. Hypervolemic: CHF, cirrhosis, nephrotic.

f: SBP prophylaxis and treatment in cirrhosis
b: Diagnosis: ascitic fluid PMN≥250/mm³. Treatment: cefotaxime IV (or ceftriaxone). After episode: lifelong secondary prophylaxis with norfloxacin/TMP-SMX. Primary prophylaxis: ascitic protein <1.5 g/dL with renal dysfunction or liver failure. SBP mortality: 20-30% per episode.

C: Confusion. U: Urea>7mmol/L (BUN>19). R: RR≥30. B: BP<90systolic or ≤60 diastolic. 65: Age≥65. Score 0-1: outpatient. 2: short hospitalization or close outpatient. 3-5: hospitalization, consider ICU if 4-5.

Definite: 2 major, 1 major+3 minor, or 5 minor. Major: 1) Positive blood cultures (typical organisms ×2, or persistent). 2) Endocardial involvement (vegetation/abscess/dehiscence on echo, new regurgitant murmur). Minor: predisposition, fever>38°C, vascular phenomena (Janeway), immunologic (Osler, Roth spots, RF+), suggestive micro.

f: Tumor lysis syndrome — prevention and treatment
b: Risk: high tumor burden, bulky lymphoma, acute leukemia with high WBC. Prevention: aggressive IV hydration, allopurinol (or rasburicase if high risk). Monitor K⁺, phosphate, uric acid, Ca²⁺, creatinine q6-8h. Treat hyperkalemia urgently. Rasburicase contraindicated in G6PD deficiency.

f: ANCA-associated vasculitis — GPA vs MPA vs EGPA
b: GPA (Wegener): c-ANCA/PR3, upper+lower respiratory tract + kidneys, granulomas, saddle nose. MPA: p-ANCA/MPO, kidneys (rapidly progressive GN) + lungs (no granulomas). EGPA (Churg-Strauss): p-ANCA/MPO (40%), asthma + eosinophilia + neuropathy. All: treat with cyclophosphamide/rituximab + steroids.

Presentation: hypotension refractory to fluids/vasopressors, hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, hypoglycemia, confusion. Often triggered by infection/stress in undiagnosed adrenal insufficiency. Treatment: dexamethasone 4mg IV (doesn't interfere with cortisol assay) + NS resuscitation. Then confirm with AM cortisol and ACTH stim test.
<90 minutes for primary PCI. If no PCI capability, fibrinolytics within 30 min (door-to-needle). Transfer for PCI if <120 min total.
I: no HF (mortality ~6%). II: crackles, S3, JVP (17%). III: pulmonary edema (38%). IV: cardiogenic shock (81%).

1. ACEi/ARB/ARNI (sacubitril/valsartan) 2. Beta-blocker (carvedilol/metoprolol succinate/bisoprolol) 3. MRA (spironolactone/eplerenone) 4. SGLT2i (dapagliflozin/empagliflozin). Start all simultaneously when possible.
C: CHF (1), H: HTN (1), A₂: Age ≥75 (2), D: DM (1), S₂: Stroke/TIA (2), V: Vascular disease (1), A: Age 65-74 (1), Sc: Sex female (1). Max 9.

f: Aortic stenosis — indications for valve replacement
b: 1. Symptomatic severe AS (angina, syncope, HF) 2. Asymptomatic with LVEF <50% 3. Undergoing other cardiac surgery 4. Very severe (Vmax >5 m/s) with low surgical risk.

f: Wells score for PE — high probability threshold
b: >4 points = PE likely → CT-PA. ≤4 = PE unlikely → D-dimer (if negative, PE excluded). Clinical signs DVT (3), PE most likely dx (3), HR >100 (1.5), immobilization/surgery (1.5), prior DVT/PE (1.5), hemoptysis (1), malignancy (1).
Confusion, Urea >7, RR ≥30, BP <90/60, age ≥65. Score 0-1: outpatient. 2: consider admission. 3-5: ICU consideration.
Acute onset + bilateral infiltrates + P/F ≤300 + not HF. Mild 200-300, Moderate 100-200, Severe <100. Low Vt (6 mL/kg IBW), Pplat <30, prone if P/F <150, conservative fluids.
Hemodynamically unstable → systemic thrombolysis (alteplase 100mg over 2h) or surgical/catheter embolectomy. Submassive (RV strain + stable): anticoagulation ± thrombolysis based on bleeding risk.
5 variables, each scored 1-3: Total bilirubin, Albumin, INR/PT, Ascites, Hepatic encephalopathy. A: 5-6 (good prognosis), B: 7-9 (moderate), C: 10-15 (poor, consider transplant).
Ascitic fluid PMN >250/mm³. Empiric ceftriaxone 2g IV + albumin (1.5 g/kg day 1, 1 g/kg day 3). Norfloxacin prophylaxis after first episode or if ascitic protein <1.5 g/dL.
1. Resuscitate (restrictive transfusion Hb >7) 2. Octreotide IV drip 3. Ceftriaxone prophylaxis 4. Emergent endoscopy with band ligation 5. If refractory → TIPS.

f: Acute pancreatitis — Ranson's criteria and treatment
b: At admission: age >55, WBC >16k, glucose >200, LDH >350, AST >250. At 48h: Hct drop >10%, BUN rise >5, Ca <8, PaO2 <60, base deficit >4, fluid sequestration >6L. Treatment: aggressive fluids (LR preferred), pain control, NPO→advance diet as tolerated.
Stage 1: Cr rise ≥0.3 or 1.5-1.9x baseline or UO <0.5 mL/kg/h for 6-12h. Stage 2: Cr 2-2.9x baseline or UO <0.5 for ≥12h. Stage 3: Cr ≥3x or Cr ≥4 or anuria ≥12h or dialysis initiation.

FENa <1%: prerenal (volume depletion, HF, hepatorenal). FENa >2%: intrinsic renal (ATN). Exceptions: contrast nephropathy and rhabdomyolysis can have low FENa. Use FEUrea (<35% prerenal) if on diuretics.

A: Acidosis (refractory metabolic). E: Electrolytes (refractory hyperkalemia). I: Ingestion (methanol, ethylene glycol, lithium, salicylates). O: Overload (refractory pulmonary edema). U: Uremia (encephalopathy, pericarditis, bleeding).

1. Cardiac membrane stabilization: calcium gluconate 10% IV (onset 1-3 min) 2. Shift K intracellularly: insulin 10U + D50 (onset 15-30 min), albuterol nebulized 3. Remove K: kayexalate, patiromer, furosemide, dialysis.

Sepsis: suspected infection + SOFA ≥2. Septic shock: sepsis + vasopressor + lactate >2. Hour-1: measure lactate, blood cultures before abx, broad-spectrum abx, 30 mL/kg crystalloid if hypotensive, vasopressors for MAP ≥65.

Definite: 2 major, or 1 major + 3 minor, or 5 minor. Major: positive blood cultures (typical organism x2 or persistent), endocardial involvement (vegetation/abscess on echo). Minor: predisposing condition, fever, vascular phenomena (Janeway), immunologic (Osler nodes, Roth spots), microbiologic evidence.

Bacterial: high WBC (PMN predominant), high protein, low glucose (<40), positive Gram stain. Viral: moderate WBC (lymphocyte predominant), normal/mildly elevated protein, normal glucose. TB/fungal: lymphocyte predominant, very high protein, very low glucose.

f: TTP vs HUS — classic pentad and treatment
b: TTP: thrombocytopenia, MAHA (schistocytes), neurologic symptoms, renal dysfunction, fever. ADAMTS13 activity <10%. Treatment: plasma exchange (NOT platelet transfusion). HUS: similar but predominantly renal. Typical (STEC/Shiga toxin): supportive. Atypical (complement-mediated): eculizumab.

Platelet drop >50% 5-10 days after heparin exposure (or <1 day if prior exposure). 4T score for pretest probability. Confirm with anti-PF4/heparin ELISA + serotonin release assay. Stop ALL heparin. Start non-heparin anticoagulant (argatroban, bivalirudin). Do NOT give warfarin until platelets >150k (risk of venous limb gangrene).

Low platelets + low fibrinogen + elevated D-dimer + prolonged PT/PTT + schistocytes on smear. Treat underlying cause. Supportive: platelets if <10k or bleeding, cryoprecipitate if fibrinogen <100, FFP if actively bleeding with prolonged PT/PTT.

Within 4.5h of symptom onset (or last known well). CT shows no hemorrhage. Exclusions: BP >185/110 unresponsive to treatment, platelets <100k, INR >1.7, recent surgery/trauma. Extended window (4.5-9h): if perfusion mismatch on CT/MRI (EXTEND trial).

1st line: lorazepam 4mg IV (repeat x1). 2nd line: levetiracetam 60mg/kg IV OR fosphenytoin 20mg PE/kg. 3rd line (refractory): midazolam or propofol drip + intubation + continuous EEG. Define: ≥5 min continuous seizure or ≥2 without recovery.

GBS: ascending weakness, areflexia, post-infectious, CSF albuminocytologic dissociation, IVIG/PLEX (no steroids). MG: fluctuating weakness worse with activity, ocular onset, AChR/MuSK antibodies, thymoma, pyridostigmine + immunosuppression.

DKA: glucose >250, pH <7.3, bicarb <18, AG elevated, ketones+. Usually T1DM. HHS: glucose >600, serum osm >320, minimal ketosis, severe dehydration. Usually T2DM elderly. Both: IV fluids + insulin + K monitoring.

Storm: tachycardia, fever, agitation, HF, AF. Burch-Wartofsky score >45. Rx: PTU → iodine (1h later) → propranolol → hydrocortisone → cooling. Myxedema: hypothermia, bradycardia, AMS, hyponatremia. Rx: IV levothyroxine + hydrocortisone (before ruling out adrenal insufficiency).

Triggers: abrupt steroid withdrawal, acute illness/surgery in patients on chronic steroids, adrenal hemorrhage (Waterhouse-Friderichsen). Treatment: hydrocortisone 100mg IV bolus → 50mg q8h + aggressive NS resuscitation. No mineralocorticoid needed acutely (hydrocortisone provides sufficient).

GPA (Wegener's): c-ANCA/PR3, upper+lower respiratory+renal. MPA: p-ANCA/MPO, renal+pulmonary (no granulomas). EGPA (Churg-Strauss): p-ANCA/MPO, asthma+eosinophilia+vasculitis. Treatment: cyclophosphamide or rituximab + glucocorticoids. Maintenance: azathioprine or rituximab.

f: Gout vs pseudogout — crystals and treatment
b: Gout: negatively birefringent needle-shaped MSU crystals. Acute: colchicine, NSAIDs, or steroids. ULT: allopurinol (target urate <6), febuxostat if allopurinol intolerant. Pseudogout (CPPD): positively birefringent rhomboid crystals. Acute: similar. No equivalent of ULT.

Septic: norepinephrine 1st line → add vasopressin 0.03 U/min → add epinephrine if refractory. Cardiogenic: norepinephrine + dobutamine (or milrinone). Anaphylactic: epinephrine IM 0.3-0.5mg q5-15min. Neurogenic: norepinephrine or phenylephrine.

Mode: volume-controlled. Vt: 6 mL/kg IBW. RR: 12-35 to maintain pH >7.30. PEEP: per FiO₂-PEEP table. Pplat goal: <30 cmH₂O. If Pplat >30 → decrease Vt to 5 or 4 mL/kg. pH <7.15 → increase RR or consider bicarb.
M: Methanol. U: Uremia. D: DKA. P: Propylene glycol/Paraldehyde. I: INH/Iron. L: Lactic acidosis. E: Ethylene glycol. S: Salicylates.

Normal <10. Elevated in: methanol, ethylene glycol, isopropyl alcohol, mannitol, propylene glycol. Key: methanol and ethylene glycol have BOTH elevated osmolar gap AND anion gap (once metabolized). Treatment: fomepizole + dialysis.
Max 8-10 mEq/L per 24h. Overcorrection → osmotic demyelination syndrome (central pontine myelinolysis). If overcorrected: D5W + desmopressin to relower sodium.

Euvolemic hyponatremia + serum osm <275 + urine osm >100 + urine Na >40 + normal thyroid/adrenal function. Causes: CNS disease, pulmonary disease, drugs (SSRIs, carbamazepine, cyclophosphamide), malignancy (SCLC).
G1: ≥90 (normal, but evidence of kidney damage). G2: 60-89. G3a: 45-59. G3b: 30-44. G4: 15-29. G5: <15 (kidney failure). Refer to nephrology at G4, start dialysis planning at G5.

f: ASCVD risk — when to start statin
b: LDL ≥190: high-intensity statin regardless. DM age 40-75: moderate-intensity (high-intensity if 10y risk ≥7.5%). 10y ASCVD risk ≥7.5%: moderate-to-high intensity. Risk enhancers: family hx, CRP ≥2, CAC >0, South Asian ancestry.

Native valve: vancomycin + ceftriaxone (or gentamicin). Prosthetic valve: vancomycin + gentamicin + rifampin. Duration: native 4-6 weeks, prosthetic 6+ weeks. Surgery indications: HF, uncontrolled infection, large vegetation (>10mm), embolic events.

f: Acute pancreatitis — etiology mnemonic (I GET SMASHED)
b: I: Idiopathic. G: Gallstones (most common). E: Ethanol (2nd most common). T: Trauma. S: Steroids. M: Mumps/Malignancy. A: Autoimmune. S: Scorpion sting. H: Hyperlipidemia/Hypercalcemia/Hypothermia. E: ERCP. D: Drugs (azathioprine, valproic acid, didanosine).

Malar rash (butterfly), discoid rash, photosensitivity, oral ulcers, arthritis (non-erosive), serositis (pleuritis/pericarditis), renal (proteinuria/casts), neurologic (seizures/psychosis), hematologic (hemolytic anemia, leukopenia, thrombocytopenia), ANA+, anti-dsDNA/anti-Smith.

Non-caseating granulomas. Bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy on CXR. Elevated ACE (neither sensitive nor specific). Hypercalcemia (1-alpha-hydroxylase in granulomas). Treatment: observation if asymptomatic; prednisone for symptomatic pulmonary/extrapulmonary. FIRE trial (P0064 article): prednisone vs MTX.

Low ferritin (<30 diagnostic), low serum iron, high TIBC, low transferrin saturation (<20%), microcytic hypochromic RBCs, elevated RDW, low reticulocyte count. Ferritin 30-100 with inflammation: check TSAT.

Massive (hemodynamically unstable): systemic thrombolysis. Submassive (stable + RV dysfunction or troponin+): close monitoring ± thrombolysis. Low-risk (stable, no RV dysfunction): anticoagulation alone, consider outpatient treatment (Hestia criteria, sPESI 0).

All ACS: aspirin 325mg loading → 81mg daily. DAPT: aspirin + P2Y12 inhibitor. STEMI PCI: ticagrelor or prasugrel preferred over clopidogrel. NSTEMI: ticagrelor or clopidogrel. Duration: minimum 12 months post-PCI (may shorten to 3-6 months with high bleeding risk).

Hepatocellular: ALT/AST >> ALP. Cholestatic: ALP/GGT >> ALT/AST. Mixed: both elevated. AST:ALT >2 → alcoholic hepatitis. Very high ALT (>1000): viral, drug/toxin (acetaminophen), ischemic, autoimmune, Wilson's, Budd-Chiari.

Corrected Ca = measured Ca + 0.8 × (4.0 - albumin). Or check ionized calcium directly (more accurate). Hypercalcemia (>10.5): dehydration, hyperparathyroidism, malignancy, granulomatous disease, thiazides, immobilization.

f: COPD GOLD classification and treatment steps
b: GOLD 1: FEV1 ≥80%. GOLD 2: 50-79%. GOLD 3: 30-49%. GOLD 4: <30%. Group A (few symptoms, low exacerbations): SABA PRN. Group B (more symptoms): LABA or LAMA. Group E (≥2 exacerbations/yr or ≥1 hospitalization): LABA+LAMA ± ICS if eos ≥300.

Provoked (surgery, immobility): 3 months. Unprovoked first event: 3-6 months then reassess (D-dimer, risk-benefit). Recurrent unprovoked: indefinite. Cancer-associated: LMWH or DOAC (edoxaban, rivaroxaban) for duration of active cancer. Apixaban extended VTE article in syllabus.

Restrictive (Hb <7): most hospitalized patients, GI bleed (stable), sepsis. Liberal (Hb <8): ACS, symptomatic anemia. Platelets: <10k prophylactic, <50k for procedures, <100k for neurosurgery/eye surgery. FFP: active bleeding with INR >1.5. Cryo: fibrinogen <100-150.

Activin signaling inhibitor (ligand trap for activin A). Approved for PAH. STELLAR trial (P0064 article): improved 6-minute walk distance + reduced NT-proBNP + improved WHO FC in PAH patients on background therapy. First-in-class mechanism.

Non-ICU: ceftriaxone + azithromycin (or respiratory fluoroquinolone alone). ICU: ceftriaxone + azithromycin (add vanc+pip-tazo if MRSA/Pseudomonas risk). Atypical coverage always needed (Legionella, Mycoplasma, Chlamydophila).
Ceftriaxone 2g IV (or cefotaxime). Add albumin 1.5 g/kg day 1, 1 g/kg day 3 (reduces hepatorenal syndrome and mortality). Diagnosed by ascitic PMN >250/mm³.

Vancomycin + ceftriaxone + ampicillin (if >50y or immunocompromised, for Listeria). Dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg q6h × 4 days — give BEFORE or WITH first antibiotic dose (reduces mortality in pneumococcal meningitis).
I: no symptoms with ordinary activity. II: symptoms with ordinary activity (slight limitation). III: symptoms with less-than-ordinary activity (marked limitation). IV: symptoms at rest.
Distributive: ↓SVR, ↑CO, ↓PCWP. Cardiogenic: ↑SVR, ↓CO, ↑PCWP. Hypovolemic: ↑SVR, ↓CO, ↓PCWP. Obstructive: ↑SVR, ↓CO, variable PCWP (↑ in tamponade, ↓ in PE).

Type 1: rapid (doubling of Cr in <2 wks), median survival 2 wks without treatment. Type 2: gradual, refractory ascites. Treatment: terlipressin + albumin (or norepinephrine + albumin, or midodrine + octreotide + albumin). Definitive: liver transplant.

Day 0-1: arrhythmia (VF/VT), cardiogenic shock. Day 1-3: pericarditis (early). Day 3-5: free wall rupture, VSD, papillary muscle rupture. Day 5-14: mural thrombus. Weeks: Dressler syndrome (late pericarditis). Months: ventricular aneurysm.

Increase INR: amiodarone, fluconazole, metronidazole, TMP-SMX, ciprofloxacin, cranberry. Decrease INR: rifampin, carbamazepine, phenytoin, St. John's wort. Vitamin K-rich foods (greens) decrease effect.

Nephrotic: proteinuria >3.5g/day, hypoalbuminemia, edema, hyperlipidemia, lipiduria, oval fat bodies. Causes: minimal change, FSGS, membranous, diabetic. Nephritic: hematuria (RBC casts), HTN, mild proteinuria, oliguria. Causes: IgA, post-strep, RPGN, lupus, ANCA.

f: Pleural effusion — Light's criteria (exudate if ANY met)
b: 1. Pleural/serum protein >0.5. 2. Pleural/serum LDH >0.6. 3. Pleural LDH >2/3 upper limit of normal serum LDH. Transudates: HF, cirrhosis, nephrotic. Exudates: infection, malignancy, PE, TB, pancreatitis.

≥5 min continuous seizure OR ≥2 seizures without return to baseline. Mortality 10-20% (higher with refractory SE). Convulsive SE is a medical emergency — brain damage begins at 30 min. Non-convulsive SE: subtle, diagnose with EEG.

↑Uric acid, ↑Potassium, ↑Phosphate, ↓Calcium (secondary to hyperphosphatemia → calcium-phosphate precipitation). Risk: high tumor burden, high proliferation (Burkitt lymphoma, ALL, large-cell). Prevention: hydration + allopurinol or rasburicase.

Bilirubin + Creatinine + INR (log scale). Range 6-40. Used for liver transplant allocation — higher MELD = higher priority. MELD >15: transplant evaluation. MELD >21: survival benefit from transplant exceeds waiting.

f: Aspirin with OAC in chronic coronary syndrome
b: P0064 Article: In stable CCS patients already on OAC for AF, adding aspirin increases bleeding without reducing ischemic events. Monotherapy with OAC alone preferred after initial dual therapy period.
P0064 Article: JAK inhibitor upadacitinib showed sustained remission in GCA, allowing glucocorticoid tapering. Novel steroid-sparing option for relapsing/refractory GCA.

f: What are the diagnostic criteria for STEMI on ECG?
b: ≥1mm ST elevation in 2 contiguous limb leads OR ≥2mm ST elevation in 2 contiguous precordial leads OR new LBBB

f: What troponin threshold indicates high-risk NSTEMI requiring urgent catheterization?
b: >5x upper limit of normal (ULN) or dynamic rise >50% baseline

f: What are the Light's criteria for exudative pleural effusion?
b: Pleural/serum protein >0.5 OR pleural/serum LDH >0.6 OR pleural LDH >2/3 upper normal serum LDH

f: What PaO2/FiO2 ratio defines severe ARDS?
b: PaO2/FiO2 <100 mmHg with PEEP ≥5 cmH2O

f: What Child-Pugh score indicates decompensated cirrhosis?
b: Class B (7-9 points) or Class C (10-15 points)

f: What MELD score threshold prioritizes liver transplant evaluation?
b: MELD ≥15 (3-month mortality >6%)

f: What eGFR defines stage 4 CKD requiring nephrology referral?
b: eGFR 15-29 mL/min/1.73m²

f: What urinary albumin level defines macroalbuminuria in diabetics?
b: Albumin-to-creatinine ratio >300 mg/g

f: What hemoglobin threshold defines severe anemia requiring urgent evaluation?
b: Hgb <7 g/dL or acute drop >2 g/dL

f: What platelet count indicates severe thrombocytopenia with bleeding risk?
b: Platelets <10,000/μL (spontaneous bleeding risk)

f: What fever pattern suggests bacterial vs viral infection in neutropenic patients?
b: Single temperature ≥38.3°C OR sustained ≥38°C for >1 hour indicates neutropenic fever

f: What procalcitonin level strongly suggests bacterial infection?
b: Procalcitonin >0.5 ng/mL (>2.0 ng/mL indicates severe sepsis)

f: What Glasgow Coma Scale score indicates severe brain injury?
b: GCS ≤8 (requires intubation and ICP monitoring)

f: What NIH Stroke Scale score indicates large vessel occlusion requiring thrombectomy?
b: NIHSS ≥6 (sensitivity 66% for LVO)

f: What HbA1c target is recommended for most diabetic adults?
b: HbA1c <7% (53 mmol/mol) for most adults

f: What TSH level indicates subclinical hypothyroidism?
b: TSH 4.5-10 mIU/L with normal free T4

f: What ejection fraction threshold defines HFrEF requiring ACE inhibitor?
b: LVEF ≤40% with optimal medical therapy

f: What NT-proBNP level rules out heart failure in acute setting?
b: NT-proBNP <300 pg/mL (age <50) or <450 pg/mL (age 50-75) or <900 pg/mL (age >75)

f: What FEV1/FVC ratio confirms COPD diagnosis?
b: Post-bronchodilator FEV1/FVC <0.70

f: What D-dimer threshold has high NPV for PE in low-risk patients?
b: D-dimer <500 ng/mL with Wells score <4 or PE unlikely

f: What ALT elevation indicates drug-induced liver injury requiring discontinuation?
b: ALT >3x ULN with symptoms OR ALT >5x ULN asymptomatic

f: What serum creatinine rise defines acute kidney injury?
b: ≥0.3 mg/dL increase within 48h OR ≥1.5x baseline within 7 days OR urine <0.5 mL/kg/h for 6h

f: What INR threshold requires vitamin K reversal in major bleeding?
b: INR >1.5 with major bleeding OR INR >10 without bleeding

f: What lactate level indicates tissue hypoperfusion in sepsis?
b: Serum lactate >2 mmol/L (18 mg/dL)

f: What glucose level defines severe hypoglycemia requiring IV dextrose?
b: Blood glucose <54 mg/dL (3.0 mmol/L) with altered mental status

f: What are the classic findings and ANCA pattern associated with Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis (GPA)?
b: Upper/lower respiratory tract involvement, glomerulonephritis, and systemic necrotizing vasculitis. c-ANCA (anti-PR3) positive in 90% of generalized disease, 60% in limited disease.

f: What are the SLE criteria that constitute neurologic involvement?
b: Seizures (without other cause), psychosis (without other cause), mononeuritis multiplex, myelitis, peripheral neuropathy, or confusional state.

f: What are the indications for uric acid lowering therapy in gout?
b: ≥2 attacks per year, tophi, CKD stage 2 or worse, or history of nephrolithiasis. Target uric acid <6 mg/dL (<5 mg/dL if tophi present).

f: What are the laboratory criteria for tumor lysis syndrome?
b: ≥2 of: uric acid ≥8 mg/dL or 25% increase, potassium ≥6 mEq/L or 25% increase, phosphate ≥4.5 mg/dL or 25% increase, calcium ≤7 mg/dL or 25% decrease.

f: What defines high-risk febrile neutropenia requiring inpatient management?
b: ANC <500, prolonged neutropenia expected (>7 days), significant comorbidities, MASCC score <21, or hemodynamic instability.

f: What are the classic clinical findings of superior vena cava syndrome?
b: Facial/upper extremity edema, dyspnea, cough, chest pain, headache, and distended neck/chest veins. Most commonly caused by lung cancer or lymphoma.

f: What are the hemodynamic parameters that distinguish cardiogenic from distributive shock?
b: Cardiogenic: Low CO, high SVR, high PCWP. Distributive: High CO, low SVR, low/normal PCWP. Both have low MAP.

f: What are the Berlin criteria for moderate ARDS?
b: PaO2/FiO2 100-200 mmHg, bilateral opacities on chest imaging, respiratory failure not fully explained by cardiac failure, PEEP ≥5 cmH2O.

f: What is the initial ventilator strategy for ARDS?
b: Low tidal volume (6 mL/kg ideal body weight), plateau pressure <30 cmH2O, PEEP ≥5 cmH2O, FiO2 to maintain SpO2 88-95%.

f: What is the antidote for acetaminophen poisoning and when should it be given?
b: N-acetylcysteine (NAC). Give if: toxic level on nomogram, >150 mg/kg ingested, or any hepatotoxicity. Most effective within 8 hours but beneficial up to 48 hours.

f: What are the classic features of anticholinergic toxidrome?
b: Hot as a hare (hyperthermia), blind as a bat (mydriasis), dry as a bone (anhidrosis), red as a beet (flushing), mad as a hatter (delirium).

f: What antidote is used for beta-blocker overdose and what is the mechanism?
b: High-dose insulin with dextrose (hyperinsulinemia-euglycemia therapy). Insulin enhances cardiac myocyte glucose uptake and contractility, overcoming beta-blockade.

f: What are the components and scoring of the Revised Cardiac Risk Index (RCRI)?
b: 1 point each: high-risk surgery, CAD, CHF, CVA/TIA, diabetes on insulin, creatinine >2 mg/dL. 0 points = 0.4% risk, 1 point = 1% risk, 2 points = 2.4% risk, ≥3 points = 5.4% risk.

f: Which cardiac medications should be continued perioperatively?
b: Beta-blockers (if chronic), ACE inhibitors/ARBs (continue vs hold controversial), statins (continue), aspirin (continue for secondary prevention unless high bleeding risk).

f: What are the perioperative management recommendations for diabetes medications?
b: Hold metformin 48h before (GFR <30) or day of surgery (GFR >30), continue basal insulin at 75-80% dose, hold sulfonylureas day of surgery, hold SGLT2 inhibitors 3 days before.

f: What is the key difference in mortality between SJS and TEN, and what scoring system predicts TEN mortality?
b: SJS has <5% mortality while TEN has 25-35% mortality. SCORTEN score predicts TEN mortality based on 7 factors: age >40, malignancy, tachycardia >120, initial detachment >10%, serum urea >10mmol/L, serum glucose >14mmol/L, and bicarbonate <20mmol/L.

f: What are the most common drugs causing SJS/TEN and what is the typical onset timing?
b: Most common: allopurinol, anticonvulsants (phenytoin, carbamazepine), sulfonamides, NSAIDs, and antibiotics (especially beta-lactams). Typical onset is 1-3 weeks after drug initiation, but can occur within days for previously exposed patients.

f: What are the criteria for anaphylaxis diagnosis according to current guidelines?
b: 1) Acute onset with skin/mucosa symptoms PLUS respiratory compromise or hypotension, OR 2) ≥2 systems involved after likely allergen exposure (skin, respiratory, GI, cardiovascular), OR 3) Hypotension after known allergen exposure.

f: What is the proper epinephrine dosing for anaphylaxis and when should it be repeated?
b: Adults: 0.3-0.5mg IM (1:1000) in anterolateral thigh. Children: 0.01mg/kg (max 0.3mg). Repeat every 5-15 minutes if no improvement. IV epinephrine (1:10,000) only for cardiovascular collapse with intensive monitoring.

f: How do you differentiate hereditary angioedema from allergic angioedema?
b: Hereditary angioedema: no urticaria, asymmetric swelling, family history, C1 esterase inhibitor deficiency, doesn't respond to antihistamines/steroids. Allergic: associated urticaria, responds to antihistamines, elevated tryptase acutely.

f: What are the classic risk factors and presentation of acute aortic dissection?
b: Risk factors: hypertension, connective tissue disorders (Marfan), bicuspid aortic valve, cocaine use. Presentation: sudden onset 'tearing' chest/back pain, pulse deficits, blood pressure differentials >20mmHg between arms, new aortic regurgitation murmur.

f: What is the size threshold for elective AAA repair and what are the surveillance intervals?
b: Elective repair: >5.5cm in men, >5.0cm in women, or expansion >1cm/year. Surveillance: <3cm every 2-3 years, 3.0-3.9cm yearly, 4.0-5.4cm every 6 months.

f: What is the maximum rate of sodium correction in hyponatremia and what are the risks of overcorrection?
b: Maximum correction: 6-8 mEq/L in first 24 hours, 18 mEq/L in 48 hours. Risk of overcorrection: osmotic demyelination syndrome (central pontine myelinolysis) causing quadriplegia, pseudobulbar palsy, and altered consciousness.

f: What are the diagnostic criteria for SIADH?
b: 1) Hyponatremia with hypoosmolality (serum osm <280), 2) Urine osmolality >100-150 mOsm/kg, 3) Euvolemic state, 4) Normal kidney, adrenal, and thyroid function, 5) No recent diuretic use.

f: Describe the WHO analgesic ladder and when to use each step.
b: Step 1: Non-opioids (acetaminophen, NSAIDs) for mild pain (1-3/10). Step 2: Weak opioids (codeine, tramadol) + non-opioids for moderate pain (4-6/10). Step 3: Strong opioids (morphine, oxycodone) + non-opioids for severe pain (7-10/10).

f: What is the morphine equivalent conversion ratio for oxycodone and fentanyl?
b: Oxycodone to morphine: 1.5:1 ratio (10mg oxycodone = 15mg morphine). Fentanyl patch: 1mcg/hr = 2mg oral morphine per day (50mcg/hr patch = 100mg oral morphine daily). Always reduce by 25-50% when switching opioids.

f: What were the key findings of the DIGIT-HF trial regarding digoxin in heart failure?
b: DIGIT-HF showed no mortality benefit from digoxin in heart failure patients in sinus rhythm, but modest improvement in symptoms and reduced hospitalizations. Benefit was greatest in patients with severe symptoms and EF <25%.

f: What did the ECLIPSE trial demonstrate about early vs. delayed anticoagulation in atrial fibrillation after stroke?
b: ECLIPSE trial showed that early anticoagulation (within 4 days) after minor stroke in AF patients was safe and effective, with no significant increase in symptomatic intracranial hemorrhage compared to delayed initiation.

f: What were the primary outcomes of the BALANCE trial comparing dual vs. triple therapy?
b: BALANCE trial demonstrated that dual antiplatelet therapy (aspirin + P2Y12 inhibitor) was superior to triple therapy (dual antiplatelet + anticoagulant) in reducing bleeding complications without significantly increasing thrombotic events in high-risk patients.

f: What mechanism of action and primary endpoint did Baxdrostat target in hypertension trials?
b: Baxdrostat is a selective aldosterone synthase inhibitor that reduces aldosterone production. Clinical trials showed significant blood pressure reduction in resistant hypertension patients, particularly those with elevated aldosterone levels, with good tolerability profile.
